subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: What embedded systems are and how they differ from desktop computing.
keyPoints: Embedded system: dedicated computer inside a product with a specific function
Examples: microwave, car ECU, smartwatch, router, medical device
Constraints: limited RAM (KB-MB), limited flash, low power, real-time requirements
Bare-metal: no OS, code runs directly on hardware
RTOS: Real-Time OS — lightweight OS with deterministic scheduling
MCU: Microcontroller Unit — CPU + RAM + Flash + peripherals on one chip
Popular families: ARM Cortex-M (STM32, NXP), AVR (Arduino), ESP32, PIC
subLevels.content: Embedded systems are purpose-built computers that control physical devices. Unlike a PC that runs general software, an embedded system is tightly coupled to its hardware — it reads sensors, drives actuators, and communicates with the physical world. Constraints are severe: a typical MCU has 64KB-512KB of Flash for code, 8KB-256KB of RAM for data, and must run on milliwatts of power from a battery. This means every byte matters. C is the dominant language because it gives hardware-level control while remaining portable. ARM Cortex-M is the industry standard architecture — billions of devices use it.

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: Embedded C fundamentals — registers, bit manipulation, and volatile.
keyPoints: volatile: tells compiler not to optimize away hardware register reads
uint8_t, uint32_t: fixed-width types — critical in embedded
Bit manipulation: SET bit n: reg |= (1<<n), CLEAR: reg &= ~(1<<n)
CHECK bit n: (reg >> n) & 1
Memory-mapped I/O: peripherals accessed like memory addresses
#define REG (*((volatile uint32_t*)0x40000000))
Bitfields: struct with : syntax for hardware register mapping

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Trace a bare-metal LED blink on STM32 step by step.
keyPoints: Step 1: Enable GPIO clock in RCC register
Step 2: Configure GPIO pin as output
Step 3: Set/clear pin in output data register
Step 4: Software delay loop
Step 5: Repeat forever in main loop
Key: always enable peripheral clock before using it

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Write a GPIO bit manipulation library in C.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 0: GPIO Bit Manipulation Library

Write a C library for GPIO pin control:

```c
// Simulate hardware registers
#include <stdint.h>
#include <stdio.h>

uint32_t GPIO_ODR = 0;   // Output data register
uint32_t GPIO_IDR = 0;   // Input data register
uint32_t GPIO_MODER = 0; // Mode register

// Functions to implement:
void gpio_set_output(uint8_t pin);  // configure pin as output
void gpio_set(uint8_t pin);         // set pin HIGH
void gpio_clear(uint8_t pin);       // set pin LOW
void gpio_toggle(uint8_t pin);      // toggle pin
uint8_t gpio_read(uint8_t pin);     // read input pin
```

Then write a test:
1. Configure pins 5 and 13 as outputs
2. Set pin 5, clear pin 13
3. Verify GPIO_ODR has bit 5 set and bit 13 clear
4. Toggle pin 5 twice — verify it returns to original state
5. Read GPIO_IDR bit 3 (simulate input=1 by setting IDR manually)
lab.solutionExplanation: GPIO control is pure bit manipulation. Set: OR with mask. Clear: AND with inverted mask. Toggle: XOR with mask. Read: shift right and AND with 1. The 1U (unsigned) prevents sign-extension bugs on 32-bit platforms. This pattern is the foundation of all embedded hardware control.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your embedded fundamentals.

quiz.q: volatile keyword in embedded C means:
options: Do not optimize — always read from hardware register directly

quiz.q: To set bit 5 of a register without affecting other bits:
uint32_t is preferred over int in embedded because:
options: Fixed-width type — same size on all platforms, critical for hardware registers

quiz.q: In a bare-metal embedded system:
options: Code runs directly on hardware with no operating system

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: UART, SPI, and I2C — the three fundamental MCU communication protocols.
keyPoints: UART: asynchronous serial — TX/RX, no clock, fixed baud rate
SPI: synchronous serial — SCLK/MOSI/MISO/CS, full duplex, fast
I2C: two-wire — SDA/SCL, multi-master/multi-slave, addressed
UART baud rate: 9600, 115200, 921600 — must match on both ends
SPI modes: CPOL/CPHA defines clock polarity and phase
I2C address: 7-bit (128 devices) or 10-bit, pulled-up lines
HAL: Hardware Abstraction Layer — portable driver API
subLevels.content: These three protocols appear in virtually every embedded system. UART is the simplest — two wires, no clock, data sent at fixed baud rate with start/stop bits. It's used for debug output, GPS modules, and Bluetooth modules. SPI is faster (MHz+) and full-duplex — the master generates clock and can read while writing. Used for fast sensors, displays, SD cards, and ADCs. I2C uses only two wires for multi-device communication — a 7-bit address identifies each device. Used for slower sensors (IMUs, temperature sensors, EEPROMs). Choosing the right protocol depends on speed requirements, pin count, and device support.

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: Protocol implementation patterns in C — UART, SPI, I2C.
keyPoints: UART TX: wait TXE (TX empty), write to DR register
UART RX: wait RXNE (RX not empty), read from DR register
SPI transfer: write to DR, wait BSY flag, read DR
I2C write: START → address+W → data → STOP
I2C read: START → address+R → NACK on last byte → STOP
Timeout: always add timeout to avoid infinite loops
Ring buffer: circular buffer for UART RX interrupt handling

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Read an I2C transaction trace for a temperature sensor.
keyPoints: I2C START: SDA falls while SCL high
Address byte: 7-bit address + R/W bit
ACK: receiver pulls SDA low for 1 clock
NACK: SDA stays high — error or end of read
STOP: SDA rises while SCL high
LM75 temp sensor: addr 0x48, register 0x00 = temperature

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Implement a ring buffer and UART driver in C.

hints: full: ((head+1) % SIZE) == tail — one slot always empty
lab.solutionExplanation: A ring buffer (circular buffer) is the standard UART receive buffer. The head pointer advances on push (ISR writes here), tail advances on pop (main loop reads here). Full when head+1==tail (one slot wasted to distinguish full from empty). Drop when full prevents overwriting unread data. volatile head/tail ensures ISR and main code see consistent values.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your peripheral protocol knowledge.

quiz.q: I2C uses how many wires?
options: 2 (SDA, SCL) — supports multiple devices on same bus

quiz.q: SPI is preferred over I2C when:
options: High speed needed (MHz+) and pin count is not limited

quiz.q: In a ring buffer, volatile is needed on head/tail because:
options: ISR and main code share them — compiler must not cache in registers

quiz.q: UART baud rate mismatch causes:
options: Garbled data — bits sampled at wrong times

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: Interrupts — the foundation of responsive embedded systems.
keyPoints: Interrupt: hardware signal that pauses main code to run ISR
ISR: Interrupt Service Routine — must be fast, no blocking
NVIC: Nested Vector Interrupt Controller (ARM Cortex-M)
Priority: lower number = higher priority (ARM convention)
Preemption: higher priority ISR interrupts lower priority ISR
Critical section: disable interrupts to protect shared data
IRQ latency: typically 12 cycles on Cortex-M (from signal to first ISR instruction)
subLevels.content: Interrupts are what make embedded systems responsive. Without interrupts, you poll — constantly checking if a button is pressed or UART data arrived. With interrupts, the hardware tells you when something needs attention. The ISR runs immediately (12 cycles on Cortex-M), does the minimum necessary work, and returns. ISRs must never block — no while() waits, no malloc(), no printf(). Use flags or ring buffers to communicate between ISR and main loop. Critical sections (disable/enable interrupts) protect shared data from corruption when both ISR and main code access it.

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: ISR implementation, critical sections, and interrupt-safe data sharing.
keyPoints: ARM ISR: void EXTI0_IRQHandler(void) — specific function name
__disable_irq() / __enable_irq() — critical section
volatile flag: shared between ISR and main — must be volatile
atomic read: read volatile variable once into local copy
NVIC_SetPriority(IRQn, priority): set interrupt priority
NVIC_EnableIRQ(IRQn): enable interrupt in NVIC
pending flag: clear interrupt pending bit in ISR or it re-fires

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Design an interrupt-driven button debouncer with timer.
keyPoints: Button press: EXTI interrupt on falling edge
Start debounce timer (20ms)
Timer ISR: check button still pressed → confirm press
Flag set: main loop processes confirmed button press
State machine: IDLE → PRESSED → DEBOUNCING → CONFIRMED
This pattern eliminates mechanical bounce completely

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Simulate an interrupt-driven system with shared data protection.

hints: volatile head/tail prevents compiler from caching values
lab.solutionExplanation: An event queue decouples ISRs from main-loop processing. ISRs push events quickly and return. The main loop pops and processes at its own pace. Critical sections (disable/enable IRQ) protect the shared head/tail when accessed from both contexts. This pattern is the foundation of event-driven embedded architectures.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your interrupt knowledge.

quiz.q: An ISR should:
options: Be as short as possible — set a flag and return

quiz.q: Critical section in embedded C is needed when:
options: ISR and main code share data — must protect from partial reads/writes

quiz.q: If you don't clear the interrupt pending flag in an ISR:
options: ISR fires again immediately after returning — infinite loop

quiz.q: NVIC stands for:
options: Nested Vector Interrupt Controller — manages interrupts on Cortex-M

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: FreeRTOS — tasks, scheduler, and synchronization primitives.
keyPoints: FreeRTOS: most popular embedded RTOS — free, open source, widely supported
Task: lightweight thread with its own stack — preemptively scheduled
Scheduler: switches tasks based on priority and state
Semaphore: signaling between tasks/ISRs (binary or counting)
Mutex: mutual exclusion — only one task at a time
Queue: message passing between tasks — thread-safe FIFO
Tick: system timer tick — default 1ms on most configs
subLevels.content: FreeRTOS runs on top of the bare metal and provides multiple concurrent tasks, each with its own stack. The scheduler runs every tick (1ms) and switches to the highest-priority ready task. Tasks communicate via queues (data transfer), semaphores (event signaling), and mutexes (shared resource protection). The key RTOS difference from bare-metal: instead of a super-loop with flags, you have tasks that block (sleep) until an event occurs. A blocked task consumes no CPU — the scheduler gives that time to other tasks. This makes complex embedded software much more manageable.

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: FreeRTOS API — task creation, queues, semaphores, and delays.
keyPoints: xTaskCreate(func, name, stack, param, priority, handle)
vTaskDelay(pdMS_TO_TICKS(100)) — delay 100ms
xQueueCreate(length, itemSize) — create queue
xQueueSend(queue, &item, timeout) — send to queue
xQueueReceive(queue, &item, timeout) — receive from queue
xSemaphoreGive/Take — signal/wait semaphore
portMAX_DELAY — block forever until event

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Trace a FreeRTOS task state machine and scheduler decisions.
keyPoints: Task states: Running, Ready, Blocked, Suspended
Running → Blocked: task calls vTaskDelay or xQueueReceive
Blocked → Ready: delay expires or queue has data
Ready → Running: highest priority ready task picked
Context switch: saves registers, loads next task registers
Idle task: runs when no other task is ready — enter low power

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Simulate a FreeRTOS producer-consumer system in C.

lab.solutionExplanation: The RTOS queue decouples producer and consumer. The producer sends at its own rate (sensor sampling). The consumer processes at its own rate. When the queue is full (capacity exceeded), the producer drops the item — in real FreeRTOS, it would block for a timeout. The circular buffer with head/tail/count tracks items efficiently.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your RTOS knowledge.

quiz.q: A FreeRTOS task in BLOCKED state:
options: Is waiting for an event (delay, queue, semaphore) — uses zero CPU

quiz.q: FreeRTOS mutex vs semaphore:
options: Mutex has ownership — only the task that takes it can give it; semaphore has no ownership

quiz.q: vTaskDelay(pdMS_TO_TICKS(100)) will:
options: Block the current task for 100ms — other tasks run during this time

quiz.q: The FreeRTOS Idle task runs when:
options: No other task is ready — good place to enter low-power sleep

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: Timers and ADC — time measurement, PWM generation, and analog input.
keyPoints: Timer: hardware counter that generates interrupts or PWM
Prescaler: divides system clock before timer counter
Auto-reload register (ARR): timer period value
PWM: Pulse Width Modulation — motor control, LED dimming
Duty cycle: percentage of time signal is HIGH
ADC: Analog-to-Digital Converter — 12-bit = 4096 levels on STM32
ADC resolution: 3.3V / 4096 = 0.8mV per step
subLevels.content: Hardware timers are the workhorses of embedded systems. A timer counts clock pulses and generates interrupts or PWM signals. For PWM motor control: set ARR for period (say 1000 for 1kHz), set CCR (capture/compare register) for duty cycle (500 = 50%). The ADC converts an analog voltage to a digital number. A 12-bit ADC reading of 2048 on a 3.3V reference means the input is approximately 1.65V. ADC is used for reading potentiometers, temperature sensors, battery voltage, and any analog sensor. Most MCUs have 12-16 ADC channels that share one ADC hardware block.

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: Timer PWM setup and ADC reading patterns.
keyPoints: Timer period = (Prescaler+1) × (ARR+1) / fCLK
PWM duty = CCR / ARR × 100%
ADC voltage = (reading / 4096) × VREF
DMA + ADC: continuous sampling without CPU involvement
Timer input capture: measure external pulse width
Output compare: generate precise timing outputs
HAL_TIM_PWM_Start: starts PWM on specific channel

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Calculate PWM frequency and duty cycle from timer register values.
keyPoints: Given: fCLK=72MHz, PSC=71, ARR=999
Timer clock = 72MHz / (71+1) = 1MHz
PWM period = 1MHz / (999+1) = 1kHz = 1ms
If CCR=0: always LOW (0% duty)
If CCR=ARR: always HIGH (100% duty)

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Implement a PWM controller and ADC averaging filter in C.

hints: average: sum all samples, divide by count
lab.solutionExplanation: PWM duty cycle = CCR/(ARR+1). For ARR=999 and 25%: CCR=250. The +1 accounts for zero-based counting. ADC averaging reduces noise by averaging N samples — essential for stable readings from noisy sensors. Mapping ADC to PWM lets a potentiometer directly control motor speed or LED brightness — a very common embedded control pattern.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your timer and ADC knowledge.

quiz.q: Timer with fCLK=72MHz, PSC=71, ARR=999 gives PWM frequency of:
ADC raw value 2048 on a 12-bit ADC with 3.3V reference equals:
ADC averaging filter is used to:
options: Reduce noise in ADC readings by averaging multiple samples

quiz.q: PWM duty cycle of 0% means:
options: Signal is always LOW — zero power delivery

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: Embedded memory management — stack, heap, flash, and memory-mapped I/O.
keyPoints: MCU memory map: Flash (code), SRAM (data), peripheral registers
Stack: grows downward, holds local variables and function call frames
Heap: dynamic allocation with malloc/free — avoid in embedded
Stack overflow: stack grows into heap or other memory — catastrophic
.data section: initialized globals — copied from Flash to SRAM at startup
.bss section: uninitialized globals — zeroed at startup
Memory pool: pre-allocated fixed-size blocks — safer than malloc
subLevels.content: Embedded memory management is critical because MCUs have very limited RAM (often 32-256KB). The stack holds local variables and grows downward from the top of SRAM. Stack overflow (local arrays too large, deep recursion) corrupts other memory silently. Dynamic allocation (malloc) is dangerous in embedded because it fragments memory over time and can fail unpredictably — use memory pools instead. The linker script (*.ld) defines the memory map: where code goes (Flash), where data goes (SRAM), where the stack is. Understanding your memory usage is essential — use the map file from the linker to see exactly where everything lives.

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: Memory pool, static allocation, and linker section attributes.
keyPoints: __attribute__((section(".ccmram"))): place in specific memory region
static: global scope but file-local visibility
const: place in Flash (read-only) — saves RAM
Memory pool: pre-allocated array of fixed-size blocks
__attribute__((aligned(4))): ensure 4-byte alignment
extern char _estack: linker symbol for stack top
Stack canary: pattern placed at bottom to detect overflow

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Analyze a memory map and identify a stack overflow risk.
keyPoints: MCU: STM32F103, 20KB SRAM, 64KB Flash
.data: 1.2KB (initialized globals copied to SRAM)
.bss: 0.8KB (zeroed globals)
Heap: 4KB (malloc pool, avoid!)
Stack: 2KB — remaining = 20-1.2-0.8-4 = 14KB available... but stack is only 2KB!
Risk: local array of 3KB would overflow 2KB stack
Fix: make large arrays static or global

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Implement a memory pool allocator and track usage.

hints: pool_alloc: find first used[i]==0, set used[i]=1, return blocks[i]
alloc_count++ on each successful alloc, free_count++ on each free
lab.solutionExplanation: A memory pool pre-allocates a fixed number of same-size blocks. Allocation finds the first free block (O(N) scan). Free marks the block available for reuse. Key advantages over malloc: no fragmentation, deterministic timing, bounded memory usage. Track alloc/free counts to detect memory leaks (alloc_count - free_count should approach 0 at steady state).

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your embedded memory management knowledge.

quiz.q: Stack overflow in embedded systems is dangerous because:
options: Stack silently corrupts adjacent memory — no error, wrong behavior

quiz.q: Memory pools are preferred over malloc() in embedded because:
options: Pools avoid fragmentation and have deterministic allocation time

quiz.q: .bss section contains:
const uint8_t table[256] in embedded C:
options: Places data in Flash — saves RAM
Creates a copy in both Flash and RAM

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: Embedded Linux — device tree, kernel drivers, and user-space interaction.
keyPoints: Embedded Linux runs on MPUs (Raspberry Pi, BeagleBone, i.MX8)
BSP: Board Support Package — Linux ported to specific hardware
Device Tree: describes hardware to kernel (DT blob = .dtb file)
Kernel module: driver loaded at runtime (insmod/modprobe)
Character device: /dev/mydevice — read/write interface to hardware
sysfs: /sys/class/ — expose hardware state to user space
mmap: map physical memory (peripheral registers) to user space
subLevels.content: Embedded Linux runs on more powerful processors (ARM Cortex-A) and provides full Linux capabilities — file system, network stack, USB, and thousands of existing drivers. The Device Tree is the glue between hardware and kernel — it describes which peripherals are connected, their addresses, and which driver to use. Writing a Linux kernel module allows custom hardware to appear as a /dev/ file. User-space applications interact with hardware through standard file I/O (open, read, write, ioctl). This is the foundation of platforms like Raspberry Pi, BeagleBone, and industrial computers.

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: Linux kernel module structure and character device driver patterns.
keyPoints: module_init/module_exit: called on insmod/rmmod
alloc_chrdev_region: dynamically allocate device number
cdev_init/cdev_add: register character device
class_create/device_create: create /dev/name entry
copy_to_user/copy_from_user: safe kernel↔user data transfer
ioctl: device-specific commands beyond read/write
platform_driver: driver for Device Tree described hardware

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Read a Device Tree snippet and understand hardware description.
keyPoints: compatible: matches driver to hardware (string must match driver)
reg: physical base address and size of registers
interrupts: IRQ number and type (edge/level)
clocks: clock source for the peripheral
status = "okay": enables this node (disabled by default)
label: shorthand name used in overlays

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Simulate a Linux character device driver in user-space C.

hints: ioctl cmd=1: all LEDs ON, cmd=0: all OFF
copy_from_user in real kernel — here just use buf directly
lab.solutionExplanation: A Linux character device exposes hardware through standard file operations. write() receives commands from user space. read() returns device state. ioctl() handles device-specific commands. In a real kernel module, copy_from_user/copy_to_user safely transfer data between kernel and user address spaces. This pattern — simple string protocol over /dev/ — is used by GPIO, LED, and custom hardware drivers.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your embedded Linux knowledge.

quiz.q: Device Tree in embedded Linux:
options: Describes hardware to the kernel so drivers can bind to devices

quiz.q: copy_from_user() in kernel driver is used because:
options: User-space and kernel-space have different address spaces — direct pointer access is unsafe

quiz.q: A platform_driver in Linux matches a hardware device when:
options: compatible string in driver matches compatible string in Device Tree node

quiz.q: sysfs (/sys/class/) is used to:
options: Expose device attributes and state to user space as files

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: Bootloaders — startup code, memory initialization, and firmware updates.
keyPoints: Bootloader: first code to run after reset — initializes hardware, jumps to app
Startup code: sets up stack pointer, copies .data, zeros .bss
Reset vector: address at 0x00000004 (ARM Cortex-M) — first instruction
Vector table: array of function pointers at 0x00000000
DFU: Device Firmware Update — update firmware over USB/UART
Secure boot: cryptographically verify firmware before running
U-Boot: most popular embedded Linux bootloader
subLevels.content: The bootloader is the unsung hero of embedded systems. On reset, the processor reads the stack pointer from 0x00000000 and the reset handler address from 0x00000004, then jumps to the reset handler. The reset handler (startup code) copies initialized data from Flash to SRAM, zeros the BSS section, and calls main(). A custom bootloader adds: checking for firmware update requests, verifying firmware integrity (CRC/hash), copying new firmware from update storage to application area, and jumping to the application. U-Boot is the standard bootloader for embedded Linux — it initializes DDR RAM, loads the kernel from SD/eMMC/network, and boots Linux.

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: Startup code, vector table, and bootloader jump patterns.
keyPoints: Vector table: array of ISR pointers placed at 0x00000000
__attribute__((section(".isr_vector"))): place in vector section
Jump to app: set MSP, jump to reset handler
CRC32: verify firmware integrity before jump
Magic word: flag in Flash to request update on next boot
SCB->VTOR: relocate vector table for app (when bootloader exists)
Watchdog: always kick before long operations

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Trace the complete boot sequence from reset to main().
keyPoints: Reset: CPU loads SP from [0x00000000], PC from [0x00000004]
Reset_Handler: copies .data, zeros .bss
SystemInit: configure clocks (PLL, etc.)
__libc_init_array: call C++ constructors
main(): application starts
Bootloader check: is update requested? If yes, DFU mode

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Implement a firmware CRC checker for bootloader use.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 7: Firmware Integrity Checker

Implement a bootloader firmware verifier:

```c
// Firmware header at start of application
typedef struct __attribute__((packed)) {
    uint32_t magic;       // 0xDEADC0DE
    uint32_t version;     // firmware version
    uint32_t size;        // firmware size in bytes
    uint32_t crc32;       // CRC32 of firmware data
    uint8_t  name[16];    // firmware name
} FirmwareHeader;

uint32_t crc32_compute(const uint8_t* data, uint32_t len);
int      fw_verify(const uint8_t* fw_data, uint32_t fw_size);
```

Test:
1. Create a test firmware header + 256 bytes of data
2. Compute CRC32 of the data
3. Store in header
4. Verify with fw_verify() → should return 1 (OK)
5. Corrupt one byte of data → fw_verify() returns 0 (FAIL)
6. Test with wrong magic → returns 0
hints: CRC32: crc ^= data[i]; then 8 iterations of (crc&1) ? (crc>>1)^poly : crc>>1
fw_verify: check magic first, then compute CRC, compare to stored
hdr->crc32 = crc32_compute(fw_code, FW_DATA_SIZE) stores computed CRC
Corrupting one byte changes CRC — detected by mismatch
lab.solutionExplanation: Firmware verification is mandatory in production bootloaders. CRC32 detects any single-bit or multi-bit error in firmware data. The firmware header contains the expected CRC — the bootloader computes the actual CRC and compares. A magic number catches uninitialized Flash (all 0xFF) or accidentally overwritten firmware. Real bootloaders also use cryptographic signatures (SHA256+RSA) for secure boot.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your bootloader knowledge.

quiz.q: On ARM Cortex-M reset, the CPU first reads:
options: Stack pointer from [0x00000000], then PC from [0x00000004]

quiz.q: The startup code copies .data section because:
options: Initialized globals must be in writable SRAM — initial values stored in Flash

quiz.q: CRC32 in a bootloader is used to:
options: Verify firmware integrity — detect corruption or tampering

quiz.q: SCB->VTOR in a bootloader context is set to:
options: The application's Flash start address — relocate vector table from boot to app

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: Embedded power management — sleep modes, low-power design, and battery optimization.
keyPoints: MCU sleep modes: Sleep, Stop, Standby — deeper = more current reduction
Run mode: ~20mA at 72MHz; Stop mode: ~2μA; Standby: ~1μA
Wake sources: RTC alarm, GPIO interrupt, LPUART, watchdog
Dynamic voltage scaling: reduce voltage at lower frequencies
Clock gating: disable peripheral clocks when not in use
DCDC vs LDO: DCDC converter more efficient at heavy loads
Energy harvesting: solar, RF, vibration — power from environment
subLevels.content: Battery life is often the primary constraint in IoT and wearable devices. A CR2032 coin cell has ~220mAh capacity. At 20mA (active MCU), it lasts ~11 hours. With proper sleep modes — MCU sleeping 99% of the time and waking every second — average current can drop to <10μA, giving years of battery life. The key techniques: enter the deepest possible sleep mode when idle, minimize wake frequency, process data quickly and return to sleep, disable all unnecessary peripherals, and reduce clock speed to the minimum needed. Every milliamp saved multiplies battery life.

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: Sleep mode entry, clock gating, and wake configuration patterns.
keyPoints: __WFI(): Wait For Interrupt — enter sleep mode (ARM instruction)
__WFE(): Wait For Event — enter sleep, wake on event
HAL_PWR_EnterSLEEPMode: STM32 HAL sleep entry
HAL_PWR_EnterSTOPMode: deep sleep, retain SRAM
HAL_PWR_EnterSTANDBYMode: deepest, only RTC/WKUP pin wake
Clock gating: RCC->APB1ENR &= ~RCC_APB1ENR_USART2EN
RTC alarm: configure for periodic wake from stop mode

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Calculate battery life for a low-power IoT sensor node.
keyPoints: Battery: 1000mAh AA cell
Active: 15mA for 10ms every 60 seconds
Sleep: 5μA for remaining 59.99 seconds
Average current calculation
Battery life estimation
Optimization: reduce active time → huge benefit

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Implement a battery life calculator and duty cycle optimizer.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 8: Battery Life Calculator

Build a power analysis tool for embedded devices:

```c
typedef struct {
    float active_current_mA;  // current when active
    float sleep_current_uA;   // current when sleeping
    float active_time_ms;     // active duration per cycle
    float cycle_period_ms;    // total cycle period
} PowerProfile;

float calc_average_current_uA(const PowerProfile* p);
float calc_battery_life_hours(const PowerProfile* p, float battery_mAh);
float calc_optimal_period_ms(float target_life_years,
                              float battery_mAh,
                              float active_mA,
                              float sleep_uA,
                              float active_ms);
```

Test:
1. IoT sensor: active=15mA/10ms, sleep=5μA, period=60s
   → average ~7.6μA, life ~15 years on 1000mAh
2. Wearable: active=50mA/100ms, sleep=10μA, period=1s
   → calculate average and life on 200mAh
3. Find optimal period for 2-year life target
hints: Optimal period: solve for T given target average current
lab.solutionExplanation: Average current = (active_time/period) × I_active + (1 - active_time/period) × I_sleep. Battery life = capacity / average_current. The key insight: reducing active duty by 2x roughly doubles battery life. The optimal period calculation inverts this formula — given a target life, what wakeup period achieves it?

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your power management knowledge.

quiz.q: Standby mode vs Stop mode on STM32:
options: Standby (~1μA) is deeper than Stop (~2μA) but loses SRAM contents
Stop loses SRAM, Standby keeps it
Stop is only for CPU, Standby for all peripherals

quiz.q: Clock gating helps power consumption by:
options: Disabling clock to unused peripherals — no switching activity = no dynamic power

quiz.q: A device active at 20mA for 1% of the time with 1μA sleep averages:
__WFI() instruction:
options: Waits for the next interrupt — enters low-power sleep mode

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: IoT connectivity — MQTT, Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRa, and Zigbee.
keyPoints: MQTT: lightweight pub/sub protocol over TCP/IP — IoT standard
Broker: central server (Mosquitto, AWS IoT) routes messages
Topic: hierarchical string like 'home/bedroom/temperature'
QoS 0/1/2: at-most-once, at-least-once, exactly-once delivery
BLE: Bluetooth Low Energy — 10-100m, ~1μA in advertising mode
LoRa/LoRaWAN: 1-15km range, ultra-low power — ideal for rural IoT
Zigbee: mesh networking — hundreds of nodes, smart home
subLevels.content: IoT connectivity determines where and how far data can travel. Wi-Fi is ubiquitous but power-hungry (50-150mA active). BLE is energy-efficient and ideal for wearables and proximity sensors. LoRa achieves incredible range (15km in rural areas) at very low power by trading bandwidth (250bps-50kbps). MQTT over any TCP/IP transport is the de facto IoT application protocol — devices publish sensor readings to topics, subscribers receive them in real time. Modern IoT platforms (AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT) all speak MQTT and provide device management, data storage, and analytics.

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: MQTT publish/subscribe, JSON payload, and ESP32 Wi-Fi patterns.
keyPoints: mqtt_connect: connect to broker with client_id
mqtt_publish: send data to topic
mqtt_subscribe: register callback for topic
JSON: lightweight data format {'temp': 25.4, 'hum': 60}
cJSON: popular C JSON library for embedded
TLS: encrypt MQTT with SSL/TLS (port 8883)
LWT: Last Will Testament — message sent if device disconnects

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Design a complete IoT sensor network architecture.
keyPoints: Edge: MCU sensors → BLE/Zigbee → Gateway
Gateway: Raspberry Pi with Wi-Fi → MQTT broker
Cloud: AWS IoT receives MQTT → Lambda processes → DynamoDB stores
Dashboard: Grafana/Node-RED visualizes time-series data
Alerts: AWS SNS sends SMS/email on threshold exceeded
OTA: AWS IoT Jobs pushes firmware updates to devices

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Implement an MQTT message serializer/parser for IoT payloads.

hints: snprintf formats the JSON: use \" for quotes inside string
sscanf parses: %15[^\"] reads string up to closing quote
Return 1 if sscanf matched 6 fields, 0 otherwise
float_eq: use fabsf(a-b) < tolerance for float comparison
lab.solutionExplanation: JSON is the de facto IoT data format — human readable, self-describing, and widely supported. snprintf efficiently builds the JSON string. sscanf parses it back. In production, use a dedicated JSON library (cJSON, jsmn) for robustness. The key embedded concern: avoid heap allocation — use fixed-size buffers and stack allocation only.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your IoT knowledge.

quiz.q: MQTT QoS 1 guarantees:
options: At-least-once delivery — message may be received multiple times

quiz.q: LoRa is chosen for IoT when:
options: Long range (1-15km) with very low power is required

quiz.q: MQTT LWT (Last Will Testament):
options: Message broker sends automatically when device disconnects unexpectedly

quiz.q: Why avoid heap allocation (malloc) in MCU IoT code:
options: malloc is not available on MCUs
Memory fragmentation causes unpredictable failures over time in long-running devices

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: Advanced RTOS patterns — priority inversion, deadlock, and real-time guarantees.
keyPoints: Priority inversion: low-priority task holds resource needed by high-priority task
Priority inheritance: mutex temporarily raises holder's priority
Deadlock: two tasks each hold a resource the other needs — neither proceeds
Watchdog: hardware timer that resets MCU if not kicked — detects deadlock/hang
Stack overflow detection: FreeRTOS can detect task stack overflow
Jitter: variation in task execution timing — affects real-time systems
Rate monotonic: scheduling theory — shorter period = higher priority
subLevels.content: Priority inversion is a subtle but dangerous RTOS bug. Mars Pathfinder (1997) experienced priority inversion in space — the high-priority communication task was starved because a low-priority science task held a mutex. FreeRTOS mutexes use priority inheritance to solve this: the mutex holder temporarily inherits the priority of the highest-priority task waiting for it. Deadlocks happen when Task A holds Mutex1 and waits for Mutex2 while Task B holds Mutex2 and waits for Mutex1. Prevention: always acquire mutexes in the same order. The watchdog timer is the ultimate safety net — if any task hangs (stuck in a loop, waiting on a broken peripheral), the watchdog resets the MCU.

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: Advanced RTOS patterns — watchdog, task monitoring, and deadlock prevention.
keyPoints: Watchdog: HAL_IWDG_Refresh(&hiwdg) must be called periodically
Task health monitor: dedicated task that checks all tasks are running
Event flags: xEventGroupSetBits / xEventGroupWaitBits
Task notification: lightweight alternative to semaphore
Timers: xTimerCreate for software timers (no hardware timer needed)
Stream buffer: byte-stream transfer (vs queue for fixed-size messages)
Task stats: uxTaskGetStackHighWaterMark — detect near-overflow

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Diagnose a priority inversion bug in a real embedded system.
keyPoints: System: high-priority comm task, medium task, low-priority log task
Problem: comm task blocked for 500ms unexpectedly
Root cause: log task holds shared_buf mutex, gets preempted by medium task
Effect: medium task runs at medium priority, blocks high-priority comm
Fix: use mutex with priority inheritance (FreeRTOS xSemaphoreCreateMutex)
Verification: comm task response time back to <10ms

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Simulate priority inversion and demonstrate the fix.

hints: With inheritance: LogTask priority raised to CommTask (P10) > CalcTask (P5)
Raised priority prevents preemption by CalcTask
Wait with inheritance = just LogTask work (3 cycles), no CalcTask delay
lab.solutionExplanation: Priority inversion causes a high-priority task to wait far longer than necessary because a low-priority task holding its resource gets preempted by a medium-priority task. Priority inheritance solves this by temporarily raising the mutex holder's priority to match the waiter's priority — preventing the medium task from preempting. FreeRTOS xSemaphoreCreateMutex() automatically implements priority inheritance.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your advanced RTOS knowledge.

quiz.q: Priority inversion occurs when:
options: A high-priority task is blocked waiting for a resource held by a low-priority task that gets preempted by a medium-priority task

quiz.q: Priority inheritance solves priority inversion by:
options: Temporarily raising the mutex holder's priority to match the waiter

quiz.q: Watchdog timer in embedded systems:
options: Resets MCU if not refreshed within timeout — detects hangs/deadlocks

quiz.q: Deadlock prevention rule:
options: Always acquire multiple mutexes in a consistent global order
Never use more than one mutex

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: What embedded interviews at top companies actually test.
keyPoints: C coding: bit manipulation, pointers, struct, function pointers
Hardware: GPIO, UART, SPI, I2C — explain and implement
RTOS: task, mutex, semaphore, queue — explain and code
Interrupts: ISR design, shared data, critical sections
Debugging: what tools (GDB, JTAG, logic analyzer, oscilloscope)
Companies: Texas Instruments, STMicroelectronics, NXP, Qualcomm, Tesla, Apple
Most common question: implement a circular buffer in C
subLevels.content: Embedded interviews test both hardware knowledge and C coding ability. You must explain protocols (UART, SPI, I2C) from first principles, write bug-free C code under time pressure, understand RTOS concepts (priority inversion is a popular topic), and debug hardware issues. Common coding questions: implement a circular buffer, bit reversal, state machine, debouncer. Common concept questions: explain volatile, explain interrupt latency, design a UART driver, explain priority inversion. Know your tools: GDB for software debugging, JTAG for hardware debugging, oscilloscope for signal integrity, logic analyzer for protocol decoding.

subLevels.title: Common Interview Patterns
subLevels.summary: Top 10 embedded coding patterns asked in every interview.
keyPoints: 1. Circular/ring buffer — the classic embedded interview question
2. Bit reversal — reverse bits of uint32_t
3. Count set bits (popcount)
4. Swap without temp variable
5. Endian swap (big↔little endian)
6. State machine implementation
7. ISR-safe flag pattern
8. Debouncer in C
9. Fixed-point multiplication
10. CRC8 computation

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Solve 5 classic embedded interview questions.
keyPoints: Q: What does volatile do? When do you use it?
Q: Explain priority inversion and how to fix it
Q: Difference between mutex and semaphore
Q: How do you debug a system that hangs randomly?
Q: Design a UART driver from scratch

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Classic embedded interview — implement a full UART driver with ring buffer.

hints: uart_write: push to tx_buf[tx_head], advance tx_head
uart_tx_isr: pop from tx_buf[tx_tail], advance tx_tail
uart_rx_isr: push received byte to rx_buf[rx_head]
lab.solutionExplanation: A production UART driver uses ring buffers for both TX and RX. Application writes to TX ring buffer; TX ISR pops bytes and sends them one at a time. RX ISR pushes received bytes to RX ring buffer; application reads at its own pace. Overflow tracking tells you if data was lost. volatile head/tail pointers prevent compiler from caching shared state between ISR and main contexts.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Embedded interview-style questions.

quiz.q: Most common embedded interview coding question:
options: Implement a circular/ring buffer in C

quiz.q: Difference between semaphore and mutex:
options: Mutex has ownership (only taker can give), semaphore has no ownership

quiz.q: Why is malloc dangerous in production embedded firmware:
options: Heap fragmentation can cause unpredictable allocation failures in long-running systems

quiz.q: When debugging a random system hang, first step is:
options: Enable watchdog to reset on hang and log the reset reason

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: Complete embedded system — the final boss challenge.
keyPoints: System design: MCU selection, memory sizing, power budget, connectivity
RTOS architecture: task decomposition, IPC design, timing analysis
Driver layer: HAL abstraction, portability across MCU families
Application layer: business logic, state machines, protocol stacks
Production readiness: watchdog, fault handlers, OTA updates, logging
Safety: ISO 26262 (automotive), IEC 62443 (industrial), MISRA-C
Testing: unit tests (Unity), hardware-in-the-loop, static analysis
subLevels.content: A production embedded system is far more than just making LEDs blink. It requires careful hardware-software co-design: choosing the right MCU for the power/performance/cost trade-off, partitioning software into well-defined layers (hardware abstraction, OS, drivers, application), designing for reliability (watchdog, fault recovery, graceful degradation), and meeting safety standards if applicable. The firmware architecture must be maintainable — clean APIs between layers, comprehensive logging, and testability from day one. OTA (Over-The-Air) firmware updates are now mandatory for any connected product — a bug in the field must be fixable without physical access.

subLevels.title: Architecture Overview
subLevels.summary: Complete embedded system software architecture.
keyPoints: Layer 1: Hardware — MCU, sensors, actuators, power
Layer 2: HAL/BSP — portable hardware abstraction
Layer 3: RTOS — FreeRTOS tasks, queues, mutexes
Layer 4: Middleware — protocol stacks, filesystems
Layer 5: Application — business logic, state machines
Layer 6: Management — OTA, logging, diagnostics
Cross-cutting: error handling, watchdog, security

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Read a complete embedded system specification and design decisions.
keyPoints: Application: industrial temperature monitor, 5-year battery life
MCU: STM32L4 (ultra-low power ARM Cortex-M4)
Connectivity: LoRa for 10km range, no Wi-Fi (power budget)
RTOS: FreeRTOS, 4 tasks
Power: avg 8μA → 5+ years on 2× AA cells
OTA: firmware updates over LoRa (slow but reliable)

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: FINAL BOSS — Complete embedded system: sensor + RTOS + MQTT + OTA.

hints: sbuf_average: sum all count items, divide by count
build_mqtt_payload: snprintf JSON with fw, temp, samples, tx, err
ota_verify: check magic + compute checksum vs stored
lab.solutionExplanation: The complete embedded system integrates all concepts: ADC sensor reading with averaging filter (noise reduction), ring buffer for sample storage, MQTT JSON serialization for IoT connectivity, OTA firmware update with integrity verification, and watchdog for system health monitoring. This architecture handles real-world embedded requirements: reliability, maintainability, and field upgradability.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Final boss — complete embedded systems architect knowledge.

quiz.q: The most important quality of production embedded firmware:
options: Reliability — must work correctly for years without maintenance

quiz.q: OTA (Over-The-Air) update requires:
options: CRC/hash verification + dual-bank flash for atomic update + rollback capability

quiz.q: MISRA-C is:
options: Coding standard for safety-critical embedded C — restricts dangerous language features

quiz.q: Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) testing:
options: Tests firmware against real or simulated hardware to catch hardware-software integration bugs
Is the same as unit testing
